systems.name: ii V I Comping System
systems.summary: The central harmonic-rhythmic system of the book, organized entirely around the ii V I progression (Dm7-G7-Cmaj7). Members are the chord voicings and named comping rhythms that operate over this progression. Traversal rules govern how a player moves between rhythmic and harmonic states - from quarter-note Freddie Green strumming through syncopated Charleston placement to chromatic approach-chord resolutions. Modification rules govern how chord symbols are colored beyond their lead-sheet representation and how approach chords transform any target voicing chromatically before resolving into it. The system's defining property is that every element is labeled, rule-governed, and sequenced - students move through named techniques in a prescribed order (Freddie Green -> Charleston -> mix; above -> below -> mix) before combining them freely.

members.name: Dm7 Chord Voicing
members.summary: The ii chord of the C major ii V I; the first chord shape students learn.

members.name: G7 Chord Voicing
members.summary: The V chord of the C major ii V I.

members.name: Cmaj7 Chord Voicing
members.summary: The I chord of the C major ii V I.

members.name: Cmaj9 Chord Voicing
members.summary: A color variant of Cmaj7, used to illustrate lead-sheet symbol enrichment.

members.name: Freddie Green Rhythm
members.summary: Quarter-note chords on every beat, with accents on beats 2 and 4 to imitate swing feel.

members.name: Charleston Rhythm
members.summary: Chord placement on beat 1 and the & of 2 in each bar.

members.name: Approach Chord From Above
members.summary: The target voicing shape moved one fret higher, resolving down into the target.

members.name: Approach Chord From Below
members.summary: The target voicing shape moved one fret lower, resolving up into the target.

traversal_rules.name: Freddie Green Quarter-Note Pulse
traversal_rules.summary: Play one chord voicing per beat across all four beats, establishing a continuous harmonic pulse through the progression.

references.quote_excerpt: The rhythm is all quarter notes, meaning you play one chord per beat.

traversal_rules.name: Freddie Green Beats-2-and-4 Accent Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Accent the chords on beats 2 and 4 to produce authentic swing feel, imitating the hi-hat.

references.quote_excerpt: accent the 24 and 4 chords/beats in each bar. This imitates the hi-hat

traversal_rules.name: Charleston Beat-1 and &-of-2 Placement
traversal_rules.summary: Place chords on beat 1 and the & of 2, producing the syncopated Charleston rhythmic feel.

references.quote_excerpt: places the first chord on beat 1 of each bar, then the second chord is on the & of 2

traversal_rules.name: Comping Rhythm Combination Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Once both named rhythms are internalized, mix Freddie Green and Charleston freely to generate original comping rather than playing either verbatim.

references.quote_excerpt: mix the Freddie Green and Charleston rhythms together to expand on this idea in your comping

traversal_rules.name: Approach Chord Mandatory Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: An approach chord - whether from above or below - must always resolve into the target chord voicing; the chromatic displacement is incomplete without the resolution step.

references.quote_excerpt: you play a chromatic chord one fret above or below the target chord
play the same shape one fret higher, then resolve into your target chord

traversal_rules.name: Approach Direction Sequence: Above -> Below -> Mixed
traversal_rules.summary: Students must practice approach chords from above first, then from below, then combine both directions - the prescribed traversal order through the three approach-chord states.

references.quote_excerpt: Start with the approach chords from above, then from below, then mix both together

modification_rules.name: Lead-Sheet Symbol Color Expansion
modification_rules.summary: A chord symbol on a lead sheet represents the minimum harmonic content; the performer must add color (e.g., reading Cmaj7 but voicing Cmaj9), reflecting real-life jazz performance convention.

references.quote_excerpt: you normally see the simplest chord symbol written on a lead sheet, then you as the performer color that chord

modification_rules.name: Approach Chord One-Fret Chromatic Displacement
modification_rules.summary: Any target voicing can be modified into an approach chord by transposing the entire shape exactly one fret up or down before resolving back; the shape itself is preserved unchanged.

references.quote_excerpt: play a chromatic chord one fret above or below the target chord

modification_rules.name: Rhythm and Picking Pattern Variation After Internalization
modification_rules.summary: Once any comping exercise is secure, the player must vary rhythms and picking patterns to self-extend the exercise beyond the written version.

references.quote_excerpt: feel free to change the rhythms and picking patterns when comfortable to expand this approach chord exercise
Work this pattern as written, then play with the rhythms and take it to other keys

systems.name: Two-Shape Major Scale Fretboard System
systems.summary: The soloing system of the book. Its foundational premise is that all three chords of a ii V I share a parent major key, so a single major scale covers the entire progression. The system delivers this through exactly two positional shapes - Shape 1 rooted on the 6th string, Shape 2 rooted on the 5th string - each memorized in isolation before being combined to cover the full neck. Traversal rules govern movement between positions and the integration of chromatic ornaments (the 134 pattern) within scale passages. The defining constraint is mandatory resolution of the 134 chromatic ornament: the four-note pattern must always complete to the third finger or the ornament sounds like an error. An earn-before-restraint philosophy governs ornament acquisition: deliberate overplay first, musical restraint only after the ear is trained.

members.name: Major Scale Shape 1 (6th-String Root)
members.summary: Positional major scale fingering anchored to the 6th string root; the first shape to memorize and apply over backing tracks.

members.name: Major Scale Shape 2 (5th-String Root)
members.summary: Positional major scale fingering anchored to the 5th string root; the second shape, completing two-position neck coverage.

members.name: 134 Chromatic Ornament Pattern
members.summary: A four-note chromatic ornament triggered whenever a 1-3-4 fingering occurs on any string, in any key, within either scale shape.

traversal_rules.name: Single Tonic Major Scale Over Entire ii V I
traversal_rules.summary: Because all three chords of the ii V I belong to the parent major key, a single tonic major scale is the correct and sufficient soloing choice across all three chord changes.

references.quote_excerpt: all 3 chords in a ii V I are in the parent key... you can use the tonic major scale to solo over all of those chords

traversal_rules.name: Shape Sequence: Individual Shape -> Combined Neck Coverage
traversal_rules.summary: Students learn each positional shape one at a time - memorize, then apply over backing track - before combining both shapes to solo across the full fretboard.

references.quote_excerpt: Start by learning these scale shapes one at a time, add them to your soloing workout, then combine them
solo over the backing track with this scale shape, then with the 6-string shape only, then mix both together

traversal_rules.name: 134 Pattern Application Trigger
traversal_rules.summary: Whenever a 1-3-4 fingering appears anywhere within a scale passage - on any string, in any key - the 134 ornament pattern is available to be inserted.

references.quote_excerpt: This pattern is used whenever you have a 134 fingering on any string, any key, and in any scale

traversal_rules.name: 134 Pattern Mandatory Resolution to Third Finger
traversal_rules.summary: The 134 ornament pattern must always complete all four notes and resolve to the third finger; an incomplete pattern sounds like a mistake, not a stylistic choice.

references.quote_excerpt: The essential item is that you resolve the pattern to the 3 finger. If you leave things hanging and don't resolve the pattern by playing all 4 notes, you sound like you made a mistake.

traversal_rules.name: Ornament Integration Sequence: Isolated Position -> Combined -> Free
traversal_rules.summary: The 134 pattern is first worked in one positional shape with metronome, then combined across both shapes technically, then applied freely over backing tracks.

references.quote_excerpt: Work this position with a metronome, then combine it in your technical studies with the 6-string shape. When ready, solo over the backing track

modification_rules.name: Earn-Before-Restraint: Deliberate Overplay of Ornament
modification_rules.summary: Students must consciously overuse the 134 ornament first to train the ear fully; only after saturation can they pull back and deploy it with musical taste.

references.quote_excerpt: overplay it in the beginning. When your ears get used to this sound, you can pull back and add the 134 pattern here and there in a more musical way

modification_rules.name: 134 Pattern Rhythmic Freedom
modification_rules.summary: The rhythmic placement of the 134 ornament is not fixed; players have wide latitude to experiment with rhythm so long as the resolution rule is respected.

references.quote_excerpt: The rhythms you use aren't that important, meaning you have a lot of freedom to experiment with rhythms when using the 134 pattern

systems.name: Mixed Solo Texture System (Chords + Single Notes)
systems.summary: The synthesis system of the book, combining harmonic accompaniment and melodic soloing into a single mixed-solo texture. This system is motivated by real-world ensemble need: in a duo or pianoless trio, the guitarist must supply harmonic space without a dedicated chord instrument. Its members are the two texture layers - chord layer and single-note layer - and the system's traversal rules govern how a player progressively loosens constraints across four discrete stages (imitate -> substitute single notes -> substitute chord rhythms -> improvise both freely). The system encodes the book's core pedagogical conviction that improvisation is acquired by dismantling a fixed model one layer at a time, not by open-ended playing from the start.

members.name: Written Mixed Solo (Imitation Model)
members.summary: The fully notated solo combining chord hits and single-note lines; the starting reference point students learn verbatim before substituting their own material.

members.name: Chord Layer
members.summary: The harmonic accompaniment component of the mixed solo - voicings and comping rhythms drawn from the ii V I comping system.

members.name: Single-Note Layer
members.summary: The melodic soloing component of the mixed solo - lines drawn from the two-shape major scale system and ornamented with the 134 pattern.

traversal_rules.name: Four-Stage Imitation-to-Improvisation Sequence
traversal_rules.summary: Students traverse the mixed solo in four stages: (1) learn as written, (2) replace single notes with own lines, (3) retain single notes while improvising chord rhythms, (4) improvise both layers freely - each stage a discrete, verifiable intermediate state.

references.quote_excerpt: learn the solo as written... replace the single notes with your own soloing lines... keep the single notes as written... replace both the chords and single notes

traversal_rules.name: Mixed Texture Ensemble Context Rule
traversal_rules.summary: The mixed chord-and-single-note texture is the required approach when the guitarist must fill harmonic space in a duo or pianoless trio context.

references.quote_excerpt: By learning how to mix chords and single notes in your solos, you're prepared to jam in a duo or pianoless trio
